skill.name: agent-memory-mcp
skill.description: Guides setup and use of an AgentMemory MCP server for persistent, searchable project knowledge.
skill.summary: Add long-term searchable memory for agent project decisions, patterns, and architecture notes.
skill.author: Amit Rathiesh

security_audit.summary: The static analysis produced many hits because SKILL.md contains Markdown command examples and inline MCP tool examples. No prompt injection, credential theft, obfuscation, or confirmed malicious intent was found, but the skill asks users to clone and run an external repository with access to a workspace path.

medium_findings.title: External Repository Installed and Executed
medium_findings.description: The setup instructions clone a GitHub repository, install npm dependencies, compile it, and run npm scripts. This is a real supply-chain and local code execution risk because the reviewed skill package does not include that external code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly documented and would execute third-party Node.js code. The risk is moderate because no malicious payload is visible in the reviewed skill file.

medium_findings.title: MCP Server Receives Workspace Path
medium_findings.description: The MCP server and dashboard are started with an absolute path to the target workspace. A memory server with that path may read or write project documentation, so users should treat it as a trusted local service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented commands explicitly pass the target workspace path to long-running services. The exact filesystem behavior depends on the external repository, which was not part of this package.

medium_findings.title: Shell Command Substitution in Example
medium_findings.description: The current-directory example uses command substitution to pass the working directory to npm. This is common shell syntax, but users should review copied commands before running them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The substitution is present and executable in a shell, but it is a benign use of pwd rather than attacker-controlled command injection.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Flags Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner reported weak cryptographic algorithm indicators, but the reviewed lines contain frontmatter text, product description, or example memory keys. No cryptographic implementation or hash use was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not contain encryption, hashing, or security-sensitive algorithm choices. The evidence supports a scanner keyword collision.

low_findings.title: Inline MCP Examples Misread as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Several static findings point to backticked MCP tool examples such as memory_search and memory_write. These are documentation examples, not shell commands or executable code in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are fenced or inline Markdown describing MCP tool calls. They do not create an execution path by themselves.

low_findings.title: Local Dashboard URL Requires Exposure Review
low_findings.description: The documentation directs users to a localhost dashboard. This is low risk when bound only to localhost, but the external dashboard implementation should be checked before use on shared machines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: A local URL is documented, but the reviewed file does not show the server binding, authentication model, or dashboard routes.

dangerous_patterns.title: Clone and Run External Code
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill depends on code from a separate GitHub repository and instructs users to run npm install and npm scripts from that checkout.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documented setup flow is explicit and reproducible. The security impact depends on the external repository contents.

dangerous_patterns.title: Service Launched Against Workspace Path
dangerous_patterns.description: The start commands pass an absolute workspace path to an MCP server and dashboard process.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path argument is clearly documented. The amount of access granted cannot be fully evaluated from this single skill file.

content.value_statement: AI agents often lose project context between sessions. This skill documents an MCP memory server workflow for saving, searching, and reviewing durable project knowledge.
actual_capabilities: Guides cloning and setup of an AgentMemory MCP server.
Documents memory_search for finding stored decisions, patterns, and tagged notes.
Documents memory_write for recording project knowledge with keys, types, and tags.
Documents memory_read for retrieving a specific memory by key.
Documents memory_stats for reviewing memory usage information.
Mentions a local dashboard for visualizing memory usage.
limitations: The actual MCP server code is hosted in an external repository.
Users must install Node.js 18 or newer before setup.
The skill does not define authentication or access controls for the dashboard.
Memory quality depends on the keys, tags, and content users choose.

use_cases.title: Keep Architecture Decisions Available
use_cases.description: Store important project decisions so future agent sessions can retrieve the reasoning quickly.

use_cases.title: Search Reusable Implementation Patterns
use_cases.description: Find prior authentication, deployment, or testing patterns before making new changes.

use_cases.title: Review Agent Memory Coverage
use_cases.description: Use memory statistics and the dashboard to see whether project knowledge is being captured.

prompt_templates.title: Find a Stored Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Search project memory for authentication patterns and summarize the most relevant results.

prompt_templates.title: Save a Decision
prompt_templates.prompt: Save this architecture decision with a clear key, decision type, useful tags, and a concise explanation.

prompt_templates.title: Recover Specific Context
prompt_templates.prompt: Read the memory entry for the current authentication design and use it to guide the next implementation step.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Memory Health
prompt_templates.prompt: Review memory statistics, identify stale or missing project knowledge, and propose specific entries to add or update.

output_examples.output: The agent returns matching memory entries, highlights the selected authentication approach, and lists related tags for follow-up searches.

output_examples.input: Save the new deployment rollback policy.
output_examples.output: The agent records a named decision with the policy summary, relevant deployment tags, and enough context for future sessions.
The agent summarizes stored memory counts and indicates whether the project has enough architecture, pattern, and decision coverage.

best_practices: Review the external repository before installing dependencies or running npm scripts.
Use stable memory keys and consistent tags for easier search.
Store decisions with enough context to explain why the choice was made.
anti_patterns: Do not store secrets, tokens, or customer data in project memory.
Do not run the MCP server against workspaces you do not trust.
Do not use vague keys that future agents cannot understand.

faq.question: What does this skill add?
faq.answer: It documents a workflow for using an AgentMemory MCP server as persistent project memory.

faq.question: Does the skill include the MCP server code?
faq.answer: No. The instructions clone the server from an external GitHub repository.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Can it search existing memories?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented memory_search tool supports queries, optional types, and optional tags.

faq.question: Can it write new memories?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented memory_write tool records keyed content with a type and optional tags.

faq.question: What security step is most important?
faq.answer: Review the external repository and npm dependencies before running the server on a real workspace.
